title: Customer Acquisition and Selling to U.S. Companies
objectives: Describe common U.S. customer acquisition channels (digital, referral, direct, partnerships)
Explain U.S. sales cycle expectations including timeline, follow-up norms, and closing patterns
Identify pricing strategy considerations for the U.S. market

contentBlocks.title: How Americans Actually Find and Buy Products
contentBlocks.content:
Finding and winning U.S. customers requires speed and systems. You've already explored product adaptation; acquisition strategy builds directly on those decisions. An Argentine B2B company spent $30,000 at a Las Vegas trade show, collected 400 cards, and waited three weeks to follow up. Nobody remembered them. A Canadian competitor followed up within 24 hours and closed 12 deals.

Here is the practical takeaway. U.S. acquisition is digital-first. B2B: LinkedIn, content marketing, events, referrals. B2C: paid ads, SEO, Amazon. Pick two or three channels.

contentBlocks.title: The U.S. Sales Cycle — Faster and More Direct Than You Expect
contentBlocks.content:
U.S. buyers expect you to get to the point: what you offer, how much it costs, what makes you different.

What does that mean for you? Typical B2B timeline:

  • First response: Same day. Three days is too late.
  • Demo: Within one week.
  • Proposal: Within 24–48 hours of first call.
  • Decision: Under $10K in 1–2 weeks. $10K–$100K in 4–8 weeks. $100K+ in 3–6 months.
  • Follow-up: Within 3 business days, then weekly.

Set up automated sequences if you cannot respond within a few hours.

contentBlocks.title: Not Following Up Fast Enough
contentBlocks.content: In many countries, a three-day response to a business inquiry is considered normal. In the U.S., if you do not respond within hours, the buyer has already contacted your competitor. A study by Harvard Business Review found that companies who responded to leads within one hour were seven times more likely to qualify the lead than those who waited even two hours. Speed does not just help. It is often the deciding factor.

contentBlocks.title: Pricing Strategy for the U.S. Market
contentBlocks.content:
Map your U.S. competitive landscape: identify 5–10 similar companies and document their pricing tiers. Three pitfalls:

  1. Pricing too low. Competitors at $25/month and you at $8 signals something is missing.
  2. No localization. Converting your home-market price to dollars is not a strategy.
  3. Hiding pricing. U.S. buyers expect transparent pricing on your website.

The bottom line? Offer a free trial or money-back guarantee. Standard in the U.S.

contentBlocks.title: Vendor Onboarding — What U.S. Companies Require From You
contentBlocks.content:
This blindsides many international founders selling B2B. The buyer says yes, then procurement sends a packet requiring documents you have never heard of.

W-9 Form

Let's put this in perspective. Provides your EIN (taxpayer ID). Any U.S. company paying you $600+/year needs it. Have a W-9 ready to send immediately.

Certificate of Insurance (COI)

Many companies require general liability insurance ($500–$2,000/year). Some also require professional or cyber liability.

Vendor Registration

Larger companies use systems (Ariba, Coupa, Oracle) requiring registration. Takes one to four weeks.

Have everything ready before you start selling.

contentBlocks.title: Before You Spend Money: Vendor Readiness Checklist
contentBlocks.content:
Before you pitch your first U.S. B2B customer, make sure you have these ready:

  • EIN (Employer Identification Number): required for the W-9 form
  • Completed W-9 form: have a PDF ready to send within minutes
  • General liability insurance: get a policy and request a sample COI from your provider
  • Professional liability insurance: required in many tech and consulting sectors
  • U.S. bank account: most procurement systems pay via ACH, not wire transfer
  • Company registration documentation . Articles of Organization, operating agreement

Missing any of these can delay a deal by two to four weeks or kill it entirely if the buyer has alternatives who are ready to go.

questions.stem: A U.S. company wants to purchase your software service for their team of 50 employees. After agreeing on pricing, their procurement department sends you a vendor onboarding packet. Which of the following will you most likely need to provide?
options: Only a signed contract and an invoice
A W-9 form, certificate of insurance, and vendor registration information
A letter of recommendation from a previous U.S. client
Proof of U.S. citizenship for your company's founders
questions.explanation: U.S. companies with formal procurement processes typically require a W-9 (tax identification), certificate of insurance (proof of liability coverage), and registration in their vendor system before they can issue payment. Having these documents ready in advance prevents delays that can stall or kill the deal.

questions.stem: A B2B software founder from Brazil collects 200 business cards at a U.S. trade show. She plans to start sending follow-up emails the following Monday, five days after the event. Her competitor followed up with personalized demo links within 24 hours. Based on U.S. sales cycle expectations, what is the most likely outcome?
options: Both founders will have equal success because the product quality matters more than follow-up speed
The Brazilian founder will have an advantage because waiting shows patience and professionalism
The competitor will close significantly more deals because U.S. buyers move forward with whoever responds fastest
Neither will close deals because trade show leads rarely convert to sales
questions.explanation: Speed is a decisive factor in U.S. sales. The Harvard Business Review study cited in this lesson found that companies responding within one hour were seven times more likely to qualify a lead. Waiting five days means most prospects have already engaged with faster competitors. In U.S. business, the first company to follow up with a clear offer has a major advantage.

questions.stem: A founder from Nigeria built an HR compliance platform and just landed a verbal "yes" from a 200-person U.S. company. The buyer says, "Great — I'll send you over to procurement." Procurement emails him a vendor onboarding packet requesting a W-9 form, a certificate of insurance, and registration in their Coupa system. He has none of these ready. He tells procurement he'll "get back to them in a couple of weeks." Meanwhile, the buyer's team is also evaluating a U.S.-based competitor. What is the most likely outcome?
options: The buyer will wait because they already committed verbally and switching vendors is too much work
Procurement will extend the deadline since international vendors commonly need extra time for U.S. paperwork
The deal stalls or dies. The competitor who has vendor documents ready closes while he is still gathering paperwork
The founder can skip vendor onboarding by invoicing the buyer directly through his Nigerian bank account
questions.explanation: A verbal "yes" is not a closed deal in U.S. B2B sales; procurement is where deals live or die. Two weeks is an eternity when a competitor already has a W-9, insurance certificate, and Coupa registration ready to submit. U.S. companies rarely extend deadlines for vendor onboarding when an alternative vendor is already qualified. This is why the lesson emphasizes having all vendor documentation prepared before you start selling, not after a buyer says yes.

contentBlocks.title: Build Your U.S. Sales Readiness Kit
contentBlocks.instructions:
Create a checklist of everything you need before making your first U.S. sale. Mark each item as "ready," "in progress," or "not started":

  1. EIN obtained . Do you have your Employer Identification Number?
  2. W-9 form completed . Is a PDF ready to send?
  3. General liability insurance . Do you have a policy? Can you request a COI?
  4. U.S. bank account . Can you receive ACH payments?
  5. Website . Is it professional, with U.S.-market pricing and messaging?
  6. CRM system . Can you track leads and follow up systematically?
  7. Response time plan . Can you respond to inquiries within a few hours during U.S. business hours?
  8. Sales materials . Do you have a one-pager, pricing page, and case studies?

For any item marked "not started," identify the specific next step and timeline to get it done. This checklist is your vendor readiness baseline for U.S. B2B sales.

contentBlocks.title: Your First 3 U.S. Customers
contentBlocks.prompt: Picture your first 3 U.S. customers. Who are they : specific companies, industries, or buyer types? Write down their names or profiles. Now pick the one channel you'll use to reach each of them (LinkedIn outreach, trade show, referral, paid ads, marketplace). Finally, what's your follow-up plan? Remember: in the U.S., if you don't follow up within 24 hours, someone else already has. Write down exactly what you'll send and when.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.content: Speed wins in the U.S. market. Respond fast, price transparently based on U.S. competitive dynamics, and follow up consistently. For B2B sales, prepare your vendor onboarding documents (W-9, insurance certificates, company registration) before you start selling. The founders who close deals in the U.S. are the ones who make it easy for buyers to say yes.
